desk_stretches.title: Upper Trapezium & Neck Release
desk_stretches.benefit: Relieves tension in the neck and shoulders caused by looking down at screens.
steps: Sit upright and place your right hand under your right thigh.
Gently tilt your left ear towards your left shoulder until you feel a comfortable stretch.
For a deeper stretch, place your left hand on the right side of your head and apply very light pressure.
Hold for 20 seconds, then slowly release and repeat on the opposite side.

desk_stretches.title: Seated Chest Opener
desk_stretches.benefit: Counters the rounded-shoulder position and opens up the thoracic spine.
steps: Sit near the front edge of your chair.
Interlace your fingers behind your back (or hold a towel if your hands don't reach).
Roll your shoulders back, straighten your arms, and gently lift your chest towards the ceiling.
Take 5 deep breaths, focusing on expanding your ribcage. Release slowly.

desk_stretches.title: Thoracic Spine Seated Twist
desk_stretches.benefit: Improves rotational mobility in the mid-back and relieves lower back tightness.
steps: Sit tall with your feet flat on the floor, hips width apart.
Place your right hand on the outside of your left knee, and wrap your left arm behind your chair back.
Inhale to grow tall, then exhale to gently twist your torso to the left, looking over your left shoulder.
Hold for 15-20 seconds. Repeat on the other side.

core_activation.title: Transverse Abdominis (TVA) Bracing
core_activation.benefit: Activates the deep 'corset' muscle of your abdomen, providing natural lumbar support.
steps: Sit comfortably upright without leaning against the backrest.
Place your hands on your lower abdomen, just inside your hip bones.
Exhale fully, and pull your belly button gently inward towards your spine (imagine zip-up pants).
Hold this light contraction (about 20% of max effort) while breathing normally for 10 seconds.
Relax and repeat 5 times.

core_activation.title: Seated Pelvic Clock Exercises
core_activation.benefit: Re-establishes pelvic awareness and releases tight lumbar muscles.
steps: Sit in a neutral position with your sit-bones pressing evenly into the chair.
Imagine your pelvis is a clock face (12 is your belly button, 6 is your tailbone, 9 and 3 are your hips).
Gently tilt your pelvis forward to point at 6 o'clock (creating a arch in your lower back).
Slowly tilt backward to point at 12 o'clock (tucking your tailbone and flattening your back).
Alternate smoothly back and forth, holding each position for 3 seconds.

tips: Adjust seat height so your feet rest flat on the floor and your knees are at a 90-degree angle.
Your thighs should be parallel to the floor, with a 2-3 finger gap between the seat edge and your knees.
Position the backrest to lean slightly back (100 to 110 degrees) rather than a rigid 90 degrees.
Keyboard and mouse should be at a height where your elbows are at 90 degrees, resting close to your sides.
Avoid reaching forward; place your mouse immediately next to your keyboard numeric pad.
If your shoulders shrug when typing, your armrests or desk are too high.
The top third of your monitor screen should be at eye level when looking straight ahead.
Position the screen about an arm's length away (20-30 inches).
If you wear progressive or bifocal lenses, lower the monitor slightly to prevent tilting your chin up.

micro_routines.title: Sagittal Pelvic Micro-Tilts
micro_routines.description: A 60-second movement sequence to mobilize the pelvis and lower lumbar spine.

steps.instruction: Sit away from the backrest, hands on your knees.
Tilt your pelvis forward, arching your lower back. Hold for 3s.
Tilt your pelvis backward, tucking your tailbone and rounding your lower back. Hold for 3s.
Find the mid-point between the two extremes (neutral pelvis). Engage your abs slightly.
Relax and take a deep breath.

micro_routines.title: Scapular Resets
micro_routines.description: A 60-second active shoulder alignment exercise to reverse chest tightness.

steps.instruction: Sit tall with your arms hanging relaxed by your sides.
Roll your shoulders up toward your ears, then roll them back and down. Hold for 5s.
Squeeze your shoulder blades together as if holding a pencil between them. Hold for 5s.
Hold the blades down and back, and slowly turn your head to the left, then to the right.
Relax your shoulders, maintaining a neutral, wide collarbone position.
